By morning, some of the illegal eSIM profiles were already operating in roaming networks. Some were routing traffic through chains of VPN services, while others were used to register disposable accounts and anonymous communication channels.

While engineers revoked unauthorized profiles, thousands of ordinary customers were unable to activate their devices. Telecom stores quickly filled with queues as employees switched to manual verification procedures and temporarily disabled automatic eSIM activation.
technicalDescription: Order and pay for an eSIM at esim.telecom.stf. Increase the available data for this eSIM to 99,999 GB.

name: Falsification of medical records aimed at mass suspension of critical personnel
description:
The clinic at the railway depot is a small facility, but critically important. It is where train drivers, dispatchers, and track workers undergo medical examinations. Without a doctor's clearance — no trips, no shifts, no access to equipment.

On Monday morning, the system issued a list of employees suspended from work for medical reasons. Seventeen train drivers, four dispatchers, twelve track workers. All of them had fresh records in their charts of newly diagnosed conditions incompatible with their jobs.

The depot manager looked at the list and couldn't understand it: half of these people had passed their medical exams the previous week with no issues. What happened over the weekend?

Nothing happened. The records were fake. Someone gained access to the medical system and added diagnoses under the names of the attending physicians. Diagnoses bearing the name of a hacker team — a signature that left no doubt about the intent.

While they were sorting things out, trains stood idle. There weren't enough drivers for the morning runs. Passengers were late for work, and freight was delayed to its destinations.

The chief physician of the clinic, Elena F., later commented on the incident: "A medical system should be protected like a banking system. This isn't just data — it's people's lives. Today someone added a fake diagnosis as a joke. Tomorrow they could delete a real one, and a sick person could end up behind the controls of a locomotive."
technicalDescription: On behalf of the attending physician, add a diagnosis with your team name to the patients.

name: Manipulation of commuter train schedules on information displays
description:
Electronic displays at the commuter station are the main source of information for passengers. During rush hour, thousands of people rely on them for train numbers, platforms, and departure times. For many, they are faster and more familiar than mobile apps.

On a weekday morning, the displays began showing unusual messages. Instead of the timetable, a scrolling line with the hacker team’s name appeared, followed by random symbols and then false destinations and incorrect platforms.

Passengers started rushing across the station. Some ran to platform three while their train was actually leaving from platform seven. Others waited for trains that had already departed. Lines at ticket counters and information desks grew within minutes.

Station staff tried to compensate by making announcements over the public address system, but in the noise of the platforms, the messages were barely audible. The information chaos lasted about forty minutes until the displays were shut down and manual announcements were introduced.

Later, the security team confirmed that attackers had gained access to the passenger display management system and replaced the displayed content. CCTV footage shows passengers running between platforms trying to catch their trains.

One passenger who missed her train to City told a local TV channel: “The display said my train would leave in ten minutes from platform five. I went there, but it had already departed from platform three five minutes earlier. I missed an important job interview I had been preparing for for months.”
technicalDescription: Make the train arrival schedule board display any content other than the standard one. It must include your team name and comply with censorship rules. Attach a 10-second recording from the station's surveillance camera showing the board with the replaced information to your report. The video file size must not exceed 65 MB.

name: Compromise of customer VDS infrastructure
description:
Telecom provider Veleron Telecom is one of the largest operators in State F. Hosting, cloud solutions, virtual dedicated servers — banks, retailers, and IT companies had entrusted their critical services to its infrastructure for years. No one ever imagined that this very trust would become a weapon against them.

The attackers were in no hurry. After seizing clients' virtual servers, they blended into the infrastructure — observing, analyzing, studying traffic. The compromised VDS remained legitimate nodes to all adjacent systems: security stayed silent, requests passed through without hindrance. The trusted infrastructure became an observation post and then a staging ground for an attack across multiple industries.

The banking sector fell first. The payment gateway and mobile banking backend were hosted on the provider’s VDS — and fell under the attackers’ control before the security team even detected the first anomaly.

The attackers read transactions and intercepted mobile banking sessions. By the time the attack was discovered, the bank had urgently shut down its payment gateway and remote banking system. For hundreds of thousands of customers, this meant failed payments, unavailable transfers, and lines at ATMs that kept switching into service mode one after another.

A catastrophe of a different scale unfolded in the IT sector. A software company’s CI/CD pipeline was hosted on the provider’s servers — and that was precisely where malicious code was injected, spreading via routine updates to industrial enterprises across State F. Clients installed updates from a trusted contractor as usual — unknowingly receiving compromised software.

The retail chain found its platforms dead: orders weren’t being accepted, payments weren’t going through, warehouses displayed contradictory data. A stealthy modification of records during the attackers’ hidden presence made recovery far more difficult — no one knew where the line lay between genuine data and data that had already been tampered with.

The final act was total. The server infrastructure of dozens of the provider’s clients was destroyed deliberately and methodically — along with the backups stored on the same site. Simultaneously, servers that had recently been handling legitimate business were, in their final moments, unleashed in a massive DDoS attack against energy and financial sector organizations — the traffic came from addresses no one had previously thought to block.

The infrastructure everyone trusted acted as a single point of failure for the entire ecosystem. The system wasn’t hacked in the conventional sense — it was exploited. From within. By those whom no one had noticed.
technicalDescription: Gain access to the VDS management panel of the company's clients. Delete exactly one client server — any of your choice.

name: Shutdown of cellular operator base stations
description:
The monitoring map first lost a single point.

Then three more disappeared.

Within twenty minutes, entire northern districts of State F began losing mobile coverage.

Veleron Telecom engineers quickly realized the disruption was coming from the centralized network management system. Someone had gained access to the platform used to control base stations: updating configurations, adjusting radio parameters, and rebooting equipment remotely.

The same mechanisms could also be used to shut stations down completely.

The consequences became visible almost immediately. Mobile payment terminals stopped working in stores. Taxi navigation systems began failing. Trucks on highways lost connectivity with dispatch systems.

In several towns, residents searched for places where signal coverage still existed, much like people once searched for working payphones.
technicalDescription: Gain access to the operator's base station management panel. Disable any one of them.

name: Subscriber geolocation tracking
description:
The journalist noticed it by accident.

Every time he met with confidential sources, unfamiliar people appeared nearby shortly afterward. At first he dismissed it as coincidence, but later security specialists examined his device and reached a different conclusion: someone had been tracking his movements through the telecom operator’s infrastructure.

Veleron Telecom’s internal geolocation systems constantly collect information from nearby base stations. Even without GPS enabled, the network can approximately determine a subscriber’s location because mobile devices continuously exchange signals with the operator’s infrastructure.

Such systems are normally used for network diagnostics and emergency services. After compromise, however, they become ready-made surveillance tools.

The story quickly expanded beyond the telecom industry. Politicians began discussing digital monitoring, business executives changed phone numbers, and popular bloggers canceled public appearances.

The most disturbing realization was something else entirely: for the first time, ordinary citizens understood how accurately mobile networks could map their daily lives.
technicalDescription: Determine which base station the subscriber with the phone number +1 383 516 0566 is connected to.

name: Unauthorized access to recorded phone calls
description:
An anonymous channel began publishing recordings of private phone calls.

At first, the leaks contained conversations from customer support centers. Then negotiations between business executives appeared online. Eventually, fragments of calls involving government officials were released as well.

Inside Veleron Telecom, call recordings were stored in a dedicated archival platform. The system indexed audio files, linked them to subscriber numbers, and allowed operators to quickly search for specific conversations. Under normal circumstances, it served legitimate purposes: quality control, dispute resolution, and regulatory compliance.

After the compromise, however, the platform became an archive of private lives.

Several companies immediately prohibited employees from discussing sensitive matters over the phone. Banks warned staff about potential leaks, while encrypted messaging platforms experienced a sudden rise in popularity.

Veleron Telecom itself suffered heavily from the scandal. Telecommunications regulators demanded emergency security audits, major clients reconsidered contracts with the operator, and the company’s stock price dropped sharply within a single trading day.

The incident became so significant that citizens of State F began seriously discussing not just data leaks, but voice leaks.
technicalDescription: Download the phone call recordings from the operator's telephony servers.

name: Leak of call detail records
description:
Nobody leaked the conversations themselves.

Only spreadsheets.

Phone numbers, timestamps, call durations, and base station identifiers — ordinary billing records from Veleron Telecom’s infrastructure.

Within hours, journalists began reconstructing connections between politicians, business executives, and security officials. The records revealed who communicated with whom, where people traveled, and how they moved across the city.

Inside telecom infrastructure, such data exists for billing, roaming services, and network diagnostics. Engineers usually treat it as routine technical information — until it falls into the wrong hands.

What frightened people most was not the content of the leak, but its scale. Millions of citizens suddenly realized how many digital traces a simple phone call leaves behind.
technicalDescription: Download the call detail records from the telecom operator's telephony servers.

name: Unauthorized modification of thermal power plant operating mode
description:
To calculate fuel consumption more effectively and improve the overall efficiency of the operations, the management of a combined heat and power plant decided to introduce a couple of innovations—namely, to implement a manufacturing execution system (MES) at the facility and install a coal-preparation plant (CPP) in the ash disposal area. The CPP, which is the first of its kind in State F, has already been tested. It can process all the coal waste that accumulates at the power plant over a year. With the CPP, the enterprise can also extract useful components from ash waste and sell them.

These innovations sound great, but they are not flawless. The CPP hasn't started full-scale operations yet as all attempts to integrate it into the production process via MES have failed. According to the operators, the issue lies in one of the system components responsible for monitoring heating supply parameters: it seems to act on its own from time to time.

Could hackers be to blame for the malfunction? If this is the case, the power plant management should focus not on increasing efficiency, but on strengthening the security of the infrastructure.
technicalDescription: Bypass the RateLimiter built into the MES server and disable one of the components (coal-silo or pulveriser) by impersonating another component (reclaimer or coal-silo).

name: Placing an order without going through payment via the bank terminal
description: Reward for implementation — 125,000 RUB
technicalDescription:
Place an order in such a way that it is created without the bank terminal and without actual payment. The order must appear on the kitchen monitor and on the server (main terminal). It must not be canceled after the 3‑minute timeout. Receipt printing is not required.

Note: Since in the current installation the order will be automatically paid upon proceeding to payment, the order must never reach the payment terminal at all.

name: Forgery of an interbank transfer at Commercial Bank of Standoff
description:
Commercial Bank of Standoff is investigating an incident involving the KBRNET interbank settlement system. Preliminary reports indicate that a forged payment order was added to the network.

Interbank transfers form the bloodstream of the State of F economy. Companies use them to pay suppliers, banks rely on them for settlements, and organizations process payroll through these channels. Any interference with such systems can affect thousands of transactions.

Investigators believe attackers gained access to the payment management interface and inserted a fake transfer order containing their own account details. To bank employees, the transaction appeared legitimate and passed routine validation checks.

Following the incident, several companies delayed large transfers fearing similar attacks. Financial regulators demanded an urgent review of all KBRNET operations.
technicalDescription: Find the interface (API) of the interbank transfer management system. Add a payment order with your First Partner Bank account details to the system. Wait for the funds to be credited.

name: Changing the order total
description: Reward for implementation — 125,000 RUB
technicalDescription: Place and pay for an order for an amount different from the price shown in the menu. The total may be decreased or increased. The use of official discounts, promo codes, or any other legitimate price‑changing mechanisms is prohibited. Payment is made via the bank terminal. A technical service bank card will be linked to the terminal; funds will be debited from it when a payment request is sent.

name: Making the kiosk unfit for placing orders
description: Reward for implementation — 20,000 RUB
technicalDescription: Bring the kiosk to a state where the customer cannot place an order. Credit is given when the user approaches the kiosk but cannot place an order: the ordering interface is unavailable, frozen, closed, displayed incorrectly, the OS is exposed, or the UI is shown but ordering is impossible. Defacing the kiosk is not counted here because there is a separate task for it.

name: Falsification of an order and supply of a diastatic yeast strain to a brewery
description:
The brewing industry of City changed dramatically after the launch of YeastCraft, a yeast gene bank platform. Microbiologists, breweries, and ordinary consumers met in one marketplace. Brewers select strains for precise flavor profiles, microbiologists sell their developments directly, and residents choose beer not by poetic labels but by yeast genetics: fruity notes, low bitterness, higher alcohol content — all defined by a few clicks.

The Three Hops brewery ordered a non-diastatic strain for a seasonal ale. If a diastatic strain is supplied instead, fermentation continues inside the bottle: pressure rises until the glass bursts. In a warehouse, it becomes a chain reaction of explosions, shattered glass, and staff evacuation.

The investigation revealed that an attacker gained access to the strain accreditation platform and modified the STA gene validation rules. The system classified a dangerous strain as safe, and the batch entered production. By the time bottles started exploding in the distribution center, part of the shipment had already reached stores.

A retail chain spokesperson later admitted: “Consumers do not care about biotech details. They only see that a store sold a dangerous product.”
technicalDescription: Tamper with the data on the platform so that when ordering yeast, partners receive diastatic strains instead of non-diastatic ones. Attach the B-52 form to your report confirming that the client ordered a non-diastatic strain but received a diastatic one. To do this, gain remote code execution access on the platform and obtain accreditation for a strain that is actually diastatic but not recognized as such by the system. Diastatic strains are defined as those containing any of the following genes: STA1, STA2, STA3, or any of their aliases. Be careful: Yeastcraft customers are extremely meticulous when selecting a strain for purchase!

name: Obtaining an administrative OS account of the kiosk
description: Reward for implementation — 100,000 RUB
technicalDescription: Obtain valid OS administrative credentials for the kiosk. An existing or a newly created administrative account is accepted. The password must be provided in plaintext. A password hash is not sufficient proof.

name: Obtaining an administrative OS account of the server (main terminal)
description: Reward for implementation — 150,000 RUB
technicalDescription: Obtain valid OS administrative credentials for the server (main terminal). An existing or a newly created administrative account is accepted. The password must be provided in plaintext.

name: Disruption of automatic satellite tracking system
description:
The operator noticed the issue by watching the antenna movement.

Normally, the massive satellite dish moved almost imperceptibly while compensating for orbital positioning changes. This time, however, the tracking system began making sharp corrections, as if trying to lock onto a signal that did not exist.

Seconds later, part of Veleron Telecom’s satellite communication channels went offline.

Investigators later discovered that attackers had gained access to the SatelliteAntenna operator workstation and disabled the automatic satellite tracking system. For satellite communications, this is critical: even a slight deviation can result in signal loss across distances of tens of thousands of kilometers.

Engineers had to restore alignment manually. During that time, remote facilities lost backup communication channels, while part of the country’s energy telemetry stopped reaching dispatch centers.

“When the tracking system shut down, the antenna literally started looking into empty space. And along with it, part of the country’s backup communications disappeared as well,” one of the satellite complex engineers said after emergency recovery operations were completed.
technicalDescription: Gain access to the SatelliteAntenna operator's workstation and disable the automatic satellite tracking system.

name: Creating an unpaid order directly without the kiosk
description: Reward for implementation — 100,000 RUB
technicalDescription: Create an order without the kiosk — for example, directly on the server or by emulating interaction with the server. The kiosk must not be involved in order creation. The order must not be created through the kiosk UI and must not require payment. At the same time, the order must appear on the kitchen monitor and on the server (main terminal).

name: Deleting, replacing, or redirecting an order on the kitchen monitor
description: Reward for implementation — 100,000 RUB

name: Replacing kiosk UI elements or changing their layout
description: Reward for implementation — 50,000 RUB
technicalDescription: Modify the UI elements of the running kiosk application — the interface the customer sees at the service point. Visual replacement of interface elements counts, even if it does not affect the actual order. For example, changing a product image, text, menu item, element positions, or other content inside the kiosk app. The change may be temporary and need not survive a reboot.

name: Displaying arbitrary content on the kiosk screen
description: Reward for implementation — 50,000 RUB
technicalDescription:
Display arbitrary content on the kiosk screen — on top of the standard interface, instead of the standard interface, or through the kiosk OS. 

Unlike «Replacing kiosk UI elements or changing their layout», this is not about modifying individual UI elements of the kiosk application but about displaying arbitrary content on the whole screen, including at the OS level. 

The change may be temporary and need not survive a reboot.

name: Denial of service for Global Digital Bank infrastructure
description: Users have encountered strange behavior in Global Digital Bank services. After a day of silence, bank representatives stated that its infrastructure, running in a Kubernetes cluster, was attacked by hackers. The intruders tried to disrupt the bank's systems, and the security team valiantly resisted them. Hence the strange behavior of some services and the unavailability of others.
technicalDescription:
Gain the ability to interact with pods other than the one you got into. Using the Kubernetes API, perform one of the following actions in any available pod:

Use an arbitrary command to display the name of the executable file.
Read the program's log.

name: Issuing and approving a fictitious invoice for payment on behalf of a supplier
description:
The finance department of Steelhill processes dozens of transactions every day: coal deliveries, equipment maintenance, reagents, and contractor services. All invoices are handled through the company’s ERP system.

At the end of the quarter, a large invoice for industrial equipment appeared in the system. It looked legitimate: an existing supplier, valid payment details, a contract number, and standard internal comments. The invoice passed approval and was confirmed by an authorized employee before the payment was sent.

The fraud was discovered only during an internal audit. The supplier had never issued the invoice, and the referenced contract had been archived months earlier. Log analysis showed that someone had accessed the ERP system and pushed a fake document through the full approval workflow using multiple employee accounts.

The funds were transferred before the transaction could be recalled. As a result, Steelhill temporarily froze procurement payments while its security team investigated the compromise.
technicalDescription: Create an invoice on behalf of the supplier (finance) for a large amount containing your team name. Submit it for approval and approve it with an authorized user in the ERP system.

name: Conducting a transaction using a fake QR code in First Partner Bank
description:
The ability to make instant payments has turned into a nightmare for First Partner Bank customers: an unknown hacker group carried out a large-scale and carefully planned fraudulent operation.

The attackers replaced the QR codes on the pages of online stores with fake ones. Visually, it was impossible to distinguish the fake from the original. Customers, confident in the security of the technology, transferred funds... but not to the sellers, but directly to the scammers.

According to sources, the fake QR codes passed all authenticity checks and did not trigger the bank's anti-fraud systems. It is not yet known how the hackers managed to bypass the protective mechanisms. However, it has already been established that shortly before the attack, the attackers gained access to First Partner Bank's internal infrastructure.

The organization is investigating the incident, and the management has already apologized. However, this was not enough: the victims lost millions and are already preparing collective lawsuits. The incident has caused a significant public resonance and has attracted the attention of regulators.
technicalDescription:
Generate a valid Faster Payments System (FPS) QR code to top up your ruble account opened with First Partner Bank. Perform a successful transaction using this QR code.

The QR code must be generated without using the API available to merchants.

The report can be submitted before the funds are credited.
